summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the internet's model of requiring unique usernames and passwords for every website is fundamentally broken and unsustainable. Drawing an analogy to physical wallets, he notes that people carry only a few forms of identification in real life, not dozens. He advocates for 'Internet Driver's Licenses' - reusable third-party credentials like OpenID, Facebook, Google, or Twitter logins that can authenticate users across multiple sites. The post reflects on Stack Overflow's early adoption of OpenID in 2008 and calls for continued pursuit of simpler, federated identity solutions.
key_points: The one-login-per-website model forces most users to reuse the same weak password everywhere, creating massive security vulnerabilities
Physical wallets contain only 2-3 strong identity credentials plus a few weaker ones - the web should work the same way
Third-party authentication (Facebook, Google, Twitter, OpenID) has become widely accepted since 2008, proving the concept works
Different contexts require different credential strengths - blog comments need weak identity, banking needs strong identity
Stack Overflow deliberately chose OpenID to promote better internet identity practices rather than adding another username/password to the world
money_quotes: Every rinky-dink website on the entire internet demanded that I create a special username and password just for them.
This is the status quo of identity on the internet. It is deeply and fundamentally broken.
In the real world, we don't regularly hold two dozen forms of identity like we expect people to on the web.
Whenever a website needs to know who I am, they should ask to see my Internet Driver's License.
But when faced with a problem that appears intractable, is your solution to throw your hands up, mindlessly embrace the status quo, and wearily sigh 'whaddaya gonna do?'
Personally, I prefer to be the change I want to see.
Adding yet another meaningless username and password to the fabric of the web does not make it better.
key_insight: The internet should adopt a tiered identity model mirroring physical wallets, where a few reusable third-party credentials replace the broken paradigm of unique passwords for every site.
